get.summary: Forward auth endpoint with OIDC flow.
get.description:
In contrast to the much more simple `/oidc/forward_auth`, which only check the `Authorization`
header for a valid JWT token, this endpoint expects an encrypted cookie and redirects to the
login / callback if invalid.

get.summary: OIDC Authorization HTML
get.description:
Starts the authorization_code flow. Log in with username / password.
If one does not exist, a new session will be opened with the 'Init' state and set's a cookie.

post.summary: POST login credentials to proceed with the authorization_code flow
post.description:
This is the 2nd step of the authorization_code flow.
The already validated params from the GET /auth/v1/oidc/authorize + Username + Password + CSRF Token + *rauthy-session* Cookie will be validated. On success, it will return an empty body but with **Location** and possibly **Allowed-Origins** header set.
If the user has MFA configured, this will be requested after a successful login via credentials first. **Permissions** - `session-init` - `session-auth`

200.description: Correct credentials, but needs to continue with Webauthn MFA Login

202.description: Correct credentials and no MFA Login required, adds Location header

401.description: Bad input or CSRF Token error

post.summary: Immediate login refresh with valid session
post.description:
This endpoint is used from the login form if an authenticated and valid session still exists
and the user wants to log in to another client. Since the session is still valid, an auth code
can be issued without further authentication checks to provide a better UX.

get.summary: JWT Token public JWKS
get.description: Returns the Json Web Key Set (JWKS) for independent validation of the signed JWT Tokens.

get.summary: Single JWK by kid
get.description: Returns a single Json Web Key (JWK) by given kid

post.summary: POST for starting an OAuth 2 Device Authorization Grant flow

get.summary: GET forward authentication
get.description:
This endpoint is very similar to the `/userinfo`, but instead of returning information about
the user inside the body, information is added to the headers with an empty body.
This makes it possible to use this endpoint in combination with a reverse proxy and can be used
for authn/authz on downstream applications via Headers.

However, header based authentication as a lot of pitfalls outside the scope of Rauthy and
you should only use it, if your downstream apps do not support OIDC natively, and you have no
other choice.

Even though forward auth can be used to check general authentication / access to an application,
it can never implement a really secure, proper way to mitigate potential CSRF Attacks. This is
something, that the downstream application would have to manage.

post.summary: The token introspection endpoint for OAuth2
post.description:
By default, this endpoint requires authorization.
You can authorize in 2 different ways:
1. `Basic` auth with `client_id:client_secret`
2. `Bearer` JWT token

If your client application can't provide any, you can disable authorization for this endpoint
by setting `DANGER_DISABLE_INTROSPECT_AUTH=true` in the Rauthy config.
Only do this, if you know what you are doing and have other ways to prevent public access to
this endpoint.

get.summary: Logout HTML page
get.description:
Returns an HTML page which can be used for logging the user out. Invalidates the session and deletes
all possibly existing refresh tokens from the database. Does an automatic logout if the
`id_token_hint` is given.

parameters.name: id_token_hint
parameters.description:
Valid `id_token` issued by Rauthy to do an RP Initiated Logout.
https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rpinitiated-1_0.html

Validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_-&?=~#!$'()*+%@]+$`

parameters.name: logout_token
parameters.description:
Logout Token used for OIDC Backchannel Logout
https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-backchannel-1_0.html#LogoutToken

Validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_-&?=~#!$'()*+%@]+$`

post.summary: Send the logout confirmation
post.description: This is the corresponding endpoint for the `GET /auth/v1/oidc/logout`

parameters.name: id_token_hint
parameters.description:
Valid `id_token` issued by Rauthy to do an RP Initiated Logout.
https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rpinitiated-1_0.html

Validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_-&?=~#!$'()*+%@]+$`

parameters.name: logout_token
parameters.description:
Logout Token used for OIDC Backchannel Logout
https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-backchannel-1_0.html#LogoutToken

Validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_-&?=~#!$'()*+%@]+$`

post.summary: Rotate JWKs
post.description:
Rotates all currently exiting JWKs (Json Web Keys) for signing new tokens. This is a manual operation currently, but this may be handled by a scheduler in the future.
When the JWKs are rotated, all newly signed tokens from that point on will use the completely random secure new JWKs. **Permissions** - rauthy_admin

post.summary: Create a new session
post.description:
You can use this endpoint to create a new session outside the `/authorize` page when logging
in. This endpoint is used inside Rauthy's integration tests internally and may be used, if you
want to create your own login or other user facing UI components.
This endpoint will return a session in `Init` state. You will need to log in and verify your
credentials, but it is a prerequisite for using the `/authorize` endpoint.

CAUTION: You will never see the returned CSRF token again - save it somewhere safe!

get.summary: OIDC sessioninfo
get.description:
Returns information about the current session. This is currently only used in the Rauthy Admin UI
and does not make much sense anywhere else. Only works with a fully authenticated session.

get.summary: Session CSRF Token
get.description:
Returns the CSRF token for the current session in exchange for a valid `access_token`.
Returning the CSRF token this way is safe, since it needs the Session Cookie + JWT Token, which
is never set as a cookie at all.

**Permissions**
- token-auth && session-auth

post.summary: The token endpoint for the OAuth2 / OIDC workflow.
post.description:
The accepted options and values depend on the clients config.
Username enumeration will be prevented and no matter if the request was successful or not, it will always take about the same amount of time, except for the first 5-7 (successful) request after a fresh restart of the application.

get.summary: The userinfo endpoint for the OIDC standard.
get.description:
Depending on the JWT token from the *Authorization* header, it will return information about
the requesting user / token.

get.summary: Returns all existing users
get.description:
This endpoint will switch from simply returning all existing users to a server side pagination
dynamically, depending on the threshold set via `SSP_THRESHOLD`.
If the response contains all existing users, the status code will be an HTTP 200.
If the backend is in server side pagination mode, it will return an HTTP 206.

**Permissions**

This Endpoint will always return an `OK` to not provide any additional attack surface.
Only if the provided E-Mail exists in the Database, a password reset E-Mail will be sent out,
otherwise it will just be ignored but still return an `OK`.

**Permissions**

get.description:

Returns the remote IP that Rauthy has extracted for this client
get.description:
During development, with `debug_assertions` enabled, this endpoint returns the full set of HTTP
headers the request comes in with. A release build though will only return the extracted
"real IP". This is useful for checking any reverse proxy configuration and making sure the
setup is correct.

AuthProviderTemplate.description: Auth Provider as template value for SSR of the Login page

scheduled.description: Validation: Unix TS in the future

disclosure_text_shown.description:
Whether the user agent has explicitly shown to the user what specific information the
IDP intends to share with the RP (e.g. "idp.example will share your name, email... with
rp.example"), used by the request permission to sign-up algorithm for new users. It is
used as an assurance by the user agent to the IDP that it has indeed shown the terms of
service and privacy policy to the user in the cases where it is required to do so.

ForwardAuthParams.description:
Params to configure the `/clients/{id}/forward_auth` endpoint.

Default values:
- danger_cookie_insecure: false
- redirect_state: 401

public.description:
If set to `true`, public READ access will be granted to all key-value pairs in this
namespace. List / GET all values is NOT allowed. Only accessing a single key directly  via
`/kv/ns/{ns}/values` is.
This makes it possible to e.g. use random key names as a kind of pre-signed URL, so you
must know the exact key in advance.

encrypted.description:
If set to `true`, the backend will encrypt the value on the application layer.
The database will only contain encrypted data. Requires more resources, but is
probably a good idea for very sensitive information.

password.description: Validation: Applies password policy - max 256 characters

id_token_hint.description:
Valid `id_token` issued by Rauthy to do an RP Initiated Logout.
https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rpinitiated-1_0.html

Validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_-&?=~#!$'()*+%@]+$`

logout_token.description:
Logout Token used for OIDC Backchannel Logout
https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-backchannel-1_0.html#LogoutToken

Validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_-&?=~#!$'()*+%@]+$`

tz.description: Validation: Valid Timezone in the format of `Europe/Berlin`

oneOf.description: Ignored for anything else than `/sessions`. Defaults to `SessionState::Auth`.

danger_auth_checked_locally.description:
Setting this means a `PamToken` will be generated without any additional user authentication
validation! Only authorization will be checked!

You MUST only set this on a client, if you did the authentication with local data, like
e.g. an SSH login with public key authentication!

password.description: Validation: Applies password policy - max 256 characters

remote_password.description:
PAM remote password - generated via account dashboard.
Works for both password-only and MFA accounts.

PamPreflightRequest.description:
Preflight request for PAM authentications to check, if the given user would be allowed to log
in to this client and if so, under which conditions.

password.description: Validation: Applies password policy - max 256 characters

force_overwrite.description: Can only be set with an active `rauthy_admin` session

redirect_uri.description: Redirect URI used after a successful reset - validation: `[a-zA-Z0-9,.:/_\\-&?=~#!$'()*+%]`

accent.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

action.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

bg.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

bg_high.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

error.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

text.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

text_high.description: Validation: 3 HSL values: deg, sat, lum

password.description: Validation: Applies password policy - max 256 characters

oneOf.description: Currently ignored - will be implemented in a future version

tz.description: Validation: Valid Timezone in the format of `Europe/Berlin`

data.description: Note: `ToSchema` does currently not exist for `webauthn_rs::prelude::PublicKeyCredential`
Note: `ToSchema` does currently not exist for `webauthn_rs::prelude::PublicKeyCredential`

WellKnown.description:
The struct for the `.well-known` endpoint for automatic OIDC discovery.

An update on this struct needs an update in `src/bin/tests/handler_generic.rs` as well.

client_id_metadata_document_supported.description:
SEP-991 / draft-jonesmichael-oauth-cimd. Signals that this AS accepts
clients identified by a Client ID Metadata Document URL (Rauthy already
implements this via `ephemeral_from_url`). ChatGPT's custom-connector UI
greys out the CIMD option until the AS discovery doc carries this flag.
